articles.title: Climate heat, storms cast shadow over April polls in Assam, West Bengal, Tamil Nadu, Kerala - The Financial World
articles.full_text:
New Delhi: As high-stakes Assembly elections approach in Assam, West Bengal, Tamil Nadu and Kerala, an unusual factor is beginning to intrude into the political narrative—extreme weather.
Nearly 17.4 crore voters are expected to step out across over 2.19 lakh polling stations this April, with close to 25 lakh election personnel on duty, many of whom will be directly exposed to harsh and unpredictable weather conditions. Unlike routine election cycles, this one coincides with an increasingly volatile pre-monsoon window, where rising heat, erratic rainfall and intense thunderstorms are no longer exceptions, but patterns.
April itself has been steadily turning hotter and more unpredictable in recent years, with 2022 recorded among the warmest on record. According to the India Meteorological Department’s latest outlook, Assam is likely to see fairly widespread rainfall and thundershowers in the run-up to polling, while West Bengal may witness scattered rain along with thunderstorms and lightning. In contrast, southern states like Tamil Nadu and Kerala are expected to face intensifying heat and humidity, raising the risk of dangerous “wet-bulb” conditions.
Experts warn this is not just about discomfort—it could influence the election itself. “These evolving climate conditions could directly impact elections in several ways, like reduced voter turnout due to heat and discomfort, heightened health risks for voters and polling personnel, and operational disruptions caused by extreme weather events,” said Mahesh Palawat, Vice President (Meteorology & Climate Change), Skymet Weather.
The warning comes against a backdrop of rising fatalities linked to extreme weather, particularly lightning and heatstroke, with recent data showing sharp spikes in states like Kerala and Assam, underscoring the growing human cost of climate extremes. Climate scientists say the shift is structural, not seasonal.
Rapid warming of the Indian Ocean is increasing atmospheric moisture and instability, creating more favourable conditions for intense pre-monsoon rainfall and extreme weather, explained Roxy Mathew Koll, Climate Scientist at the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology. The Election Commission, however, has been factoring in some climate realities. “Keeping temperature rise in mind, voting starts early and continues till evening. Measures like polling station tracking and home voting options are aimed at ensuring climate conditions do not impede voters,” said O. P. Rawat, former Chief Election Commissioner of India.
Still, the scale of the challenge is raising fresh questions over preparedness—especially in rural and climate-vulnerable regions where infrastructure gaps can amplify disruptions. For now, political parties remain focused on rallies, alliances and voter arithmetic. But on the ground, another variable is quietly building.
This election, the real disruptor may not just be political opposition—but the weather itself. And it raises a larger question: Is India’s election machinery truly climate-ready?
